description_long: This object is a traditional Japanese torii gate, commonly found at the entrance to Shinto shrines. It features two vertical cylindrical pillars with a slightly weathered red finish, each anchored by a round stone base. The top crossbeam is thick, black, and gently curved, showing signs of age and wear with visible patches of discoloration. A secondary horizontal support beam connects the two pillars below the main crossbeam, adding structural stability. The overall construction appears robust, with a combination of wood and stone materials, and the surface is rough and matte, indicating exposure to the elements. The torii gate is a symbolic structure, often serving as a spiritual threshold in urban parks or temple precincts.
description: A weathered red and black Japanese torii gate with cylindrical pillars, stone bases, and a curved top crossbeam, showing signs of age and outdoor exposure.
description_view_0: Front view showing both red pillars, stone bases, and the full curved black crossbeam.
description_view_1: Side view revealing the narrow profile of the gate and the thickness of the crossbeam.
description_view_2: Rear view similar to the front, displaying the red pillars, stone bases, and the back of the curved crossbeam.
description_view_3: Opposite side view, again showing the slim profile and the depth of the crossbeam.
